text: During a laparoscopic myomectomy, multiple myomas are identified, with three on the anterior uterine wall and one large myoma on the posterior wall. Vasopressin is injected at the base of the anterior myoma, causing a color change due to transient ischemia. A myoma screw provides anterior traction, and a harmonic scalpel excises the subserous myoma, which is then deposited into the posterior cul-de-sac. Another anterior subserous myoma is similarly excised and enucleated using suction irrigation. The third myoma on the posterior wall is injected with vasopressin and excised longitudinally with minimal muscle damage. Suction irrigation aids in enucleating the softer myoma by providing a good dissection plane. The supraumbilical port is used for optimal field of vision and working space.

The surgeon carefully removes a myoma from the posterior wall using a hook, ensuring minimal muscle injury by avoiding rapid and forceful avulsions. Two small myomas are visible on the posterior wall, which will also be excised using the hook technique.

The surgeon uses stone holding forceps to remove a myoma from the anterior wall near the ostea, cutting it longitudinally to minimize bleeding. The uterus is now free of myomas, and suction and irrigation are performed. Suturing begins with a mattress suture at the base using a 35 mm endoskeen needle and number one vicryl, aiming to obliterate the dead space.

The surgeon uses a Clark knot pusher to apply an extracorporeal square knot, approximating the muscle and obliterating the space. The suture is cut slightly long for the assistant's use. Continuous suturing with a Dundee jamming knot and Aberdeen termination is performed, starting with an intracorporeal starter knot to approximate the margins and control bleeding.

The surgeon completes the locking blanket suture with multiple bites, terminating the first Dundee jamming knot using a surgeon's knot. The suture is tightened and cut short. A Western knot is then applied extracorporeally to approximate the remaining structure, feeding the suture and sliding it into place.

The surgeon approximates the serosal edges anteriorly to prevent bleeding. Suction irrigation is used to remove blood from the surgical field. The process of morcellation begins using a saw-shaped Indian morcellator to gradually morcellate all the myomas.

The surgeon uses stone holding forceps to remove small pieces of the broken myoma from the surgical field. Subsequently, a suture passer is employed to close the 15 mm port site with vicryl sutures, preventing incisional hernia. The suture is pushed inside, caught, pulled out, and tied to secure the closure.
